1.description: Lender control rights and the financial accelerator This figure presents impulse response functions of key prices and quantities to a one-standard-deviation negative TFP shock and a one-standard-deviation perfectly correlated positive risk shock in our benchmark model (“Benchmark,” the dashed black line) and in a financial accelerator model without loan covenants (“No Covenant,” the dotted black line). See Table 1 for the parameters used in the model computation.

2.description: Risk shocks and the financial accelerator In this figure, we study how risk shocks allow our model to generate business cycle variation and mitigation effects of the financial accelerator. The figure presents impulse response functions of key prices and quantities to a one-standard-deviation negative TFP shock in our benchmark model (“Benchmark,” the dashed black line) and in a financial accelerator model without loan covenants (“No covenant,” the dotted black line). We shut down risk shocks by fixing σt0 and σt1 at their steady-state levels. See Table 1 for the parameters used in the model computation.

3.description: Covenant strictness: time series This figure illustrates the time-series and business cycle properties of average covenant strictness in our sample (equally weighted across all firms). Strictness is the average value of firm-level strictness (defined as in Internet Appendix B.1) across all firms in a given quarter. Quarterly U.S. Real GDP growth comes from the Federal Reserve Bank of St Louis’ website. The sample starts with the first quarter of 1996 and ends with the last quarter of 2017.

4.description: Covenant violations and stock returns This figure plots average annualized excess returns against a firm’s lagged distance from the Debt-to-EBITDA covenant threshold (the most common covenant in DealScan). Observations to the right of the vertical dashed line correspond to covenant violations. Each circle represents average excess returns in one of the 28 equally spaced distance bins around the threshold. The solid black lines represent fitted values of local polynomial regressions on both sides of the threshold, and the dotted black lines represent 95% confidence intervals for these estimates (using bootstrapped standard errors). The sample starts with the first quarter of 1996 and ends with the last quarter of 2017.
